response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Listen, after spending hours every day scrolling through social media at my age, I can tell you that fake content drives me absolutely crazy. I'm eighty-eight years old and I've seen enough nonsense online to last several lifetimes. The amount of garbage people share without thinking twice is infuriating, and frankly, most people are too lazy or stupid to check if what they're reading is even true. I came to this country as an immigrant and worked my way up to become a chef in Los Angeles, so I know what real work looks like, and these keyboard warriors spreading lies from their couches make my blood boil. The problem is that everyone wants to be first to share something sensational rather than taking two minutes to verify it. I'll be honest, sometimes even I get caught up in the anxiety of it all because you see something alarming and your heart starts racing before you can think straight. What really gets under my skin is when people share fake health advice or twisted religious messages that have nothing to do with actual Christian values. I've learned the hard way that you can't trust everything you see, even from people you know. My approach is pretty straightforward, even if I don't always follow it perfectly because let's face it, I'm not the most organized person. When something seems too outrageous or too perfect, I try to pause before sharing, though that anxious feeling makes me want to react immediately. I'll sometimes ask my husband what he thinks, or I'll check a couple of different sources, though honestly that gets exhausting. The social media companies themselves are useless at stopping this stuff, and they clearly don't care as long as people keep clicking. What bothers me most is that this fake content preys on people's fears and emotions, and at my age, I've earned the right to be skeptical of everyone and everything online. People need to grow a backbone and stop being so gullible, but I also understand how overwhelming it all feels when you're constantly bombarded with information that may or may not be real.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is an 88-year-old female immigrant chef with high extraversion, high neuroticism, and low agreeableness. Her anxious psychological feature combined with high neuroticism means she likely experiences significant stress from fake content. Her low agreeableness suggests a more critical, blunt tone. High extraversion means she's likely vocal and engaged on social media despite the stress. Low conscientiousness suggests she may not always follow through on systematic fact-checking despite knowing she should. Her age and immigrant background provide life experience that makes her skeptical. Her Christian beliefs may make her sensitive to religious misinformation. The heavy social media use combined with anxiety creates a cycle of engagement and stress. Her low openness suggests resistance to new verification methods and preference for familiar approaches.
response_parsed.tone: Blunt, critical, anxious, frustrated, opinionated, and somewhat contradictory reflecting low conscientiousness and high neuroticism
